nativeRange.habitat: Shallow tropical reef environments, reef slopes, and lagoons; encrusts over rock and rubble substrate

decorPreferences: open placement with spacing from neighbors
stable base for frag plug or rock

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via symbiotic zooxanthellae. Supplemental target feeding of small zooplankton or phytoplankton is not generally required, though colonies in low-nutrient systems may benefit from occasional additions of dissolved organic matter or micro-plankton.

goodWith: other soft corals with adequate spacing
avoidWith: aggressive LPS with long sweeper tentacles
aggressive encrusting corals that can overgrow them

summary: Clavularia sp. (clove/glove polyps) is a soft octocoral that encrusts over rock surfaces, producing distinctive eight-tentacled polyps. Generally considered beginner-friendly; thrives under low-to-moderate light and gentle flow.
careNotes:
Clavularia sp. is discussed at genus level in the aquarium trade because husbandry requirements are broadly consistent across species. The genus contains approximately 69 accepted species with a worldwide tropical-to-polar marine distribution; aquarium specimens are typically Indo-Pacific in origin (commonly Indonesia or Australia).

Taxonomic note: LiveAquaria lists Clavularia under family Xeniidae, but current taxonomy places it in family Clavulariidae (order Malacalcyonacea, formerly Stolonifera/Alcyonacea). Wikipedia and the World Register of Marine Species confirm Clavulariidae as the accepted family.

Care notes:
• Lighting: Generally advised to keep under low-to-moderate intensity (commonly cited as ~50–150 PAR). These polyps tend to close under excessively bright light and have been reported thriving under standard T5, LED, or PC fixtures at moderate output. Placement depth can be adjusted based on fixture intensity.
• Flow: Low-to-moderate, indirect flow is generally preferred. Direct laminar flow tends to cause polyps to retract; gentle random current allows polyps to extend fully and captures dissolved nutrients.
• Placement: As an encrusting coral, Clavularia can be placed essentially anywhere in the aquarium — bottom, middle, or live rock. New frags placed on rock or frag plugs will encrust and spread across available substrate. Keep spacing from neighboring corals as colonies can overgrow adjacent species via encrustation.
• Aggression: Low-to-moderate. Clavularia does not possess sweeper tentacles and is generally peaceful in direct contact terms, but like many soft corals it may engage in chemical warfare (allelopathy) with neighboring corals, particularly in close proximity. Susceptible to being stung by more aggressive LPS corals.
• Feeding: Primarily photosynthetic via zooxanthellae. Supplemental direct feeding is not typically required; healthy colonies in reef systems with normal biological activity generally receive adequate dissolved nutrients. Some hobbyists report improved polyp extension when small amounts of zooplankton are added to the water column.
• Growth: Tends to encrust rapidly in established reef aquariums under good conditions. Colonies can spread across rockwork and may overgrow neighboring corals if not managed.
• Care disagreement: LiveAquaria rates care level as 'Moderate' (citing sensitivity to stinging from aggressive neighbors), while WWC rates it 'Easy'. The consensus across hobbyist community and pre-known requirements is beginner-level, with the main care consideration being adequate spacing from aggressive corals.
• Temperature disagreement: LiveAquaria gives 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C), WWC gives 72–76°F (22.2–24.4°C). An averaged range of 22–25°C (72–77°F) is used here.
• Minimum tank size: These are small encrusting colonies suitable for nano reef aquariums. A 20-gallon (76 L) minimum is commonly cited; the main constraint is stable reef chemistry rather than physical space.
breedingNotes: Propagation via fragging is straightforward and widely practiced. Colonies can be divided by cutting or breaking off sections of encrusted rock or frag plug; fragments readily reattach and continue encrusting. Sexual reproduction occurs via broadcast spawning in the wild but is not commonly practiced in captivity.

additional.name: World Wide Corals – Clove Polyps (multiple listings)
additional.notes: Cross-checked care level (Easy), lighting (Medium), flow (Low), and temperature (72–76°F). WWC rates care as Easy vs. LiveAquaria Moderate; beginner adopted as consensus. Temperature averaged: LiveAquaria 72–78°F vs. WWC 72–76°F → 22–25°C adopted. WWC product pages lack detailed water chemistry parameters (salinity, pH, dKH, Ca, Mg) — LiveAquaria values used for those.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Clavularia (taxonomy)
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy only: confirms family Clavulariidae (not Xeniidae as listed on LiveAquaria), order Malacalcyonacea, genus established by Blainville 1830. Also confirms common names star polyps and clove polyps, and ~69 accepted species with worldwide marine distribution including Indo-Pacific tropical species.
